text: When I began doing robotic surgery, I was much, much, much better doing laparoscopic surgery. So what you have here is a diagrammatic representation of what my laparoscopic ascending colectomy port placement looked like. And I knew that when I first began doing this, I was much better laparoscopically. So if I ever screwed something up robotically, I could always bail myself out laparoscopically.

So I wanted to respect this port configuration as much as possible. So in my lap port placement, I would put the camera right at the umbilicus. My right hand would go in the patient's left upper quadrant. And my laparoscopic left hand would go in the patient's left lower quadrant. And if I ever needed an assist port, which I rarely did, but it would go in the patient's right lower quadrant. So when I began doing XI robotics, I simply added one port, which is right there in the left midclavicular line. And that actually is a staple port here.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, beginning with encircling and dividing the ileocolic vessels. The duodenum is identified and freed, followed by dissection of the liver to create a posterior defect for retroperitoneal visibility. The hepatic colic ligament is divided, mobilizing the right colon and the white line of Toldt down to the cecum using cautery or vessel sealer. The terminal ileum mesentery and terminal ileum are divided, followed by the transverse mesocolon and transverse colon.
